answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is evidence that “interest match” by itself is not a huge determinant of happiness/job satisfaction**. But the stronger conclusion is not “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It is more like:

> People are happier in jobs with autonomy, competence/growth, good colleagues, meaning, manageable stress, and fair pay. “The topic is maximally interesting” helps, but it is only one component, and its marginal value may be smaller than people expect once the job is already pretty good.

### What the research roughly says

#### 1. Interest fit predicts satisfaction, but only moderately

Vocational-psychology research on Holland/RIASEC “person–environment fit” asks whether people are happier when their job matches their interests. Meta-analyses generally find **small-to-moderate correlations** between interest congruence and job satisfaction/well-being — often in the rough range of **r ≈ .1–.3**, depending on how fit is measured.

Representative sources:

- Assouline & Meir, 1987, meta-analysis on congruence and well-being.
- Spokane, Meir & Catalano, 2000, review of person–environment congruence.
- Tsabari, Tziner & Meir, 2005, updated meta-analysis on congruence and satisfaction.
- Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012, on vocational interests and work outcomes.

The practical implication: **interest fit matters, but it does not explain most of the variance**. Two jobs can differ in “interestingness” while other factors — autonomy, manager quality, stress, team, career trajectory — dominate your actual day-to-day happiness.

#### 2. Work design matters a lot: autonomy, feedback, skill variety, meaning

A broader literature on job design finds stronger and more reliable links between satisfaction and things like:

- Autonomy.
- Skill variety.
- Task significance.
- Feedback.
- Social support.
- Role clarity.
- Manageable workload.

A major meta-analysis is Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, on work design. This literature suggests that what makes a job satisfying is not merely “is the subject matter fascinating?” but “does the job let me exercise agency, grow, feel competent, get feedback, and work with good people?”

For your case, leading a small team could score highly on autonomy, growth, significance, influence, and skill variety — even if the technical topic is less fascinating.

#### 3. Job satisfaction has a dispositional/stable component

There is also evidence that people’s job satisfaction is partly stable across jobs. For example, Staw & Ross, 1985, and later dispositional/job-satisfaction work found that some people tend to be more or less satisfied across different roles. Twin studies, e.g. Arvey et al., suggest a nontrivial heritable/dispositional component.

This does **not** mean job choice is irrelevant. It means the “perfectly interesting job” may not transform happiness as much as imagined.

#### 4. People adapt to job changes

Longitudinal work on job changes often finds a “honeymoon-hangover” pattern: satisfaction rises around a move, then partially declines as the new job becomes normal. See Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy, 2005, for example.

So if Google feels exciting because of title, salary, leadership, brand, novelty, etc., some of that may fade. Conversely, if OpenAI feels special because the work is uniquely interesting, some of that may also be something you are already adapted to and thus undervaluing.

#### 5. Money helps, but at high income the marginal happiness effect is usually not huge

At high incomes, “a bit higher salary” often matters less for day-to-day happiness than job quality, time, stress, and autonomy — unless the money materially changes your security, family options, ability to buy a house, visa situation, etc.

So I would treat the salary bump as real but probably not decisive unless it changes something concrete in your life.

### How I would translate this to your situation

I would not ask:

> Which job is more interesting?

I would ask:

> Which job gives me the better whole bundle of autonomy, growth, people, meaning, lifestyle, and future optionality?

A useful distinction:

- **“This work is less glamorous/less cutting-edge, but I will have more ownership, learn management, build a team, and still care about the mission.”**  
  That could easily be happier and better for you.

- **“This work is genuinely boring to me, I will lose technical edge, the team lead role is mostly process/coordination, and I am taking it for money/title.”**  
  That is much riskier.

Also: if leading a team is “something you always wanted to do,” that is not merely extrinsic. It may be a deep source of intrinsic motivation. Management can be intellectually interesting in a different way: strategy, judgment, mentorship, leverage, organizational design.

### Practical decision test

Before deciding, I would try to answer these concretely:

1. **What would my weekly calendar actually look like?**  
   Percent research/engineering, people management, meetings, planning, bureaucracy.

2. **How much autonomy would I have?**  
   Can I choose direction, hire, set standards, influence roadmap?

3. **How good is the manager/org?**  
   A great manager/team can make a less interesting project good; a bad org can ruin a fascinating one.

4. **Will I be learning something I genuinely want to learn?**  
   E.g. technical leadership, management, product sense, scaling teams.

5. **Is the Google move reversible?**  
   Could you return to more interesting research later? Would the team-lead experience increase optionality?

6. **Can you get the missing thing where you are?**  
   If the main draw is leading a team, can OpenAI offer a path to that? If the main draw is salary, can that be adjusted? If the main draw is management experience, can you get a tech lead role first?

### Bottom line

The evidence does **not** say “choose the most interesting job possible and you’ll be much happier.” It says something more nuanced:

> Interest matters, but after a threshold, day-to-day happiness is often driven as much or more by autonomy, growth, social environment, stress, meaning, and fit with your preferred working style.

So I would **satisfice on interestingness** — make sure the Google work is interesting enough that you will not feel bored or resentful — and then optimize over the broader bundle. If the Google role gives you real leadership, autonomy, good people, and growth, it may be worth serious consideration. If it is mainly more money plus less inspiring work, I’d be much more cautious.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is research consistent with “don’t just pick the most interesting job,” but not with “interesting work doesn’t matter.”** The best-supported view is more like:

> Interestingness / intellectual fit matters, but it is only one component of work happiness. Autonomy, competence/growth, relationships, manager/team quality, meaning, workload, and future optionality often matter as much or more. Also, people are bad at forecasting how much one salient feature—“the work is fascinating” or “the salary is higher”—will affect their day-to-day happiness.

### What the research roughly says

1. **Interest fit predicts satisfaction, but modestly**
   Research on vocational interests/person–environment fit—e.g. Holland-style “does this work match my interests?”—usually finds **positive but not huge** correlations with job satisfaction, often around the low-to-mid `.20s` in meta-analyses. That means interest congruence matters, but it explains only a modest fraction of variance in satisfaction.

   Translation: choosing work you find interesting helps, but “most interesting possible” is not a magic happiness maximizer.

2. **Broader job fit matters more than topical interest**
   Broader person–job/person–organization fit—values, autonomy, ability fit, culture, manager, work style—has stronger associations with satisfaction. Meta-analyses such as Kristof-Brown et al. find broad fit to be substantially related to job satisfaction and commitment.

   In your case, “leading a small team” is not a minor variable. It could satisfy autonomy, competence, status, impact, mentoring, and growth needs—things that are very predictive of work satisfaction.

3. **Autonomy, mastery, relationships, and meaning are very robust predictors**
   The job-characteristics and self-determination literatures repeatedly find that people tend to be happier and more motivated when they have:
   - autonomy/control,
   - opportunities to use and develop skill,
   - feedback and a sense of progress,
   - task significance/meaning,
   - good colleagues and manager support.

   These are not the same as “the technical problem is maximally fascinating.”

4. **People overpredict the effect of one salient job feature**
   Affective forecasting research—Wilson, Gilbert, Kahneman’s “focusing illusion,” etc.—suggests people often overweight the thing currently in focus. If you are comparing OpenAI vs Google, your mind may fixate on “interestingness,” “salary,” or “team lead title,” but your lived experience will be ordinary Tuesdays: meetings, reviews, debugging, politics, mentoring, deadlines, commute, sleep, manager quality.

5. **Job changes often have a honeymoon effect**
   There is evidence for a “honeymoon-hangover” pattern after job changes: satisfaction often rises after a move, then partially reverts as novelty fades. That does not mean moves are bad; it means you should ask whether the new role has durable sources of satisfaction, not just a shiny title or temporary excitement.

### So how I’d interpret this for your situation

I would not ask: **“Which job is more interesting?”**

I would ask: **“Is the less-interesting job still above my minimum threshold of intellectual/mission engagement, and does it beat the current job on durable satisfaction drivers?”**

For you, the key tradeoff seems to be:

- **OpenAI:** more interesting research/content, likely stronger intellectual excitement.
- **Google:** slightly higher salary, small-team leadership, maybe more autonomy/management growth, but less interesting work.

The leadership part could be very important. If you have “always wanted” to lead a small team, that may represent a real underlying preference: mentoring, setting direction, having leverage, building taste in others, owning outcomes. That can be deeply satisfying even if the object-level technical problem is less sexy.

But there is a danger too: “leading a team” can mean very different things. It might mean genuine technical leadership and people development; or it might mean meetings, roadmaps, perf calibration, cross-org alignment, and less hands-on research. You need to know which.

### Questions I’d clarify before deciding

Ask Google, very concretely:

1. **What would my calendar look like in a normal week?**
   Percent time on coding/research, design docs, 1:1s, project management, hiring, cross-functional meetings.

2. **How much real autonomy would I have?**
   Can you set the technical agenda, or are you executing someone else’s roadmap?

3. **What makes this team important?**
   Is the work less interesting but high-impact? Or less interesting and also less meaningful?

4. **Who is my manager, and how good are they?**
   Manager quality is a huge happiness variable.

5. **Is this a real management/leadership path?**
   Or a temporary “tech lead with responsibility but no authority” situation?

6. **What are the exit options after 12–24 months?**
   Would this open doors you want? Could you return to frontier research? Would you become pigeonholed?

Also ask yourself:

- If the work were only “pretty interesting,” would I still be excited to go in because of the team/leadership/growth?
- Would I feel I had abandoned work I find uniquely meaningful?
- Am I attracted to Google because of leadership, or because I want recognition/control that I might be able to get at OpenAI too?
- Could I negotiate for more leadership responsibility at OpenAI instead?

### My practical takeaway

The research does **not** support a simple rule like “choose the most interesting job.” It supports something more like:

> Choose a job that is interesting enough, with high autonomy, good people, meaningful impact, growth, and sustainable daily life.

So if the Google role is **still intellectually acceptable** and the leadership opportunity is real, it could be a very rational move—even if the work is less interesting. But if “less interesting” really means “I will not care about this work,” then the salary/title premium probably will not compensate for that for long.

A good heuristic: **set a floor, not a maximum, for interestingness.** Once a role clears that floor, optimize for autonomy, people, growth, meaning, and future optionality.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is evidence that “interest fit” matters, but it is not nearly as dominant as career folklore suggests.** The best reading is: don’t choose a job you find boring or meaningless, but also don’t assume that the most intellectually interesting job will make you the happiest. Autonomy, manager quality, coworkers, workload, career growth, identity, and whether the day-to-day work fits your temperament can matter as much or more.

A few relevant findings:

| Finding | Rough implication |
|---|---|
| **Vocational interest fit predicts job satisfaction, but modestly.** Meta-analyses of interest–job congruence generally find correlations around the **small-to-moderate** range, often roughly `r ≈ .2–.3`, depending on measurement. See e.g. Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow; Hoff et al. on interest fit and job satisfaction. | Being interested in the domain helps, but it explains only a minority of why people are satisfied. |
| **Broader person–job fit predicts satisfaction more strongly.** Meta-analyses of person–job/person–organization fit find larger associations with satisfaction, but these include more than “interestingness”: abilities, values, needs, work style, culture, rewards, etc. Kristof-Brown et al. 2005 is a classic meta-analysis. | “Is this role a good fit for me?” is more important than “Is this topic maximally fascinating?” |
| **Job design features matter a lot.** Autonomy, feedback, task significance, skill variety, social support, and reasonable demands are consistently related to satisfaction and motivation. Hackman & Oldham; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007. | A less glamorous project with real autonomy and ownership can feel better than a fascinating project where you have little control or are constantly stressed. |
| **People are bad at affective forecasting.** Work by Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman, etc. suggests people overweight salient differences when making decisions and underweight repeated daily experiences. | When deciding, “frontier AI research!” or “team lead!” may loom larger than the actual Tuesday-at-10am experience. |
| **Income helps, but at high incomes marginal effects are usually not huge.** Recent work suggests happiness keeps rising with income for many people, but roughly logarithmically and with heterogeneity. At your likely compensation level, “a bit higher salary” probably matters less than role quality unless it changes your freedom/security materially. | Don’t ignore salary, but a modest raise probably should not dominate. |

So the empirical answer is not “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It is more like:

> **Interestingness is a real factor, but people often over-treat it as the single master variable. Once the job is above some threshold of meaningful/engaging, other dimensions may dominate happiness.**

For your specific choice, I would separate three questions:

1. **Is the Google role merely less intellectually cutting-edge, or actually boring to you?**  
   If it is “less frontier but still worthwhile,” the leadership opportunity may compensate. If you expect to feel daily indifference toward the work, be careful.

2. **Do you want to lead, or do you want to be a researcher who happens to have authority?**  
   Leading a small team means more planning, people management, ambiguity, alignment, performance conversations, recruiting, and less hands-on technical work. If that sounds intrinsically interesting to you, then the Google offer is not necessarily “less interesting”; it is interesting along a different axis.

3. **Which role gives you more autonomy and growth over the next 2–3 years?**  
   A less sexy project where you own direction, mentor people, and build a track record as a lead could be very satisfying. Conversely, if the “lead” title is mostly coordination without real decision rights, the appeal may be weaker.

A useful test: imagine an ordinary week six months from now.

- In the OpenAI role: what are you doing Monday–Friday? What frustrates you? What gives you energy?
- In the Google role: how much of your time is technical judgment, mentoring, setting direction, meetings, stakeholder management, execution? Do you enjoy that version of yourself?

My practical read: **do not optimize for “most interesting possible.” Optimize for “interesting enough + high autonomy + good people + sustainable pace + role that builds the person I want to become.”** If leading a team is something you genuinely want and the Google role has real authority, it may be worth taking seriously even if the technical content is less thrilling. If the Google work fails the “I can care about this for years” test, then the extra salary and title may not make up for it.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is evidence suggesting that “choose the most interesting work possible” is an over-simple rule**. But the stronger claim — “interesting work doesn’t matter much” — is not supported either.

A better summary of the research is:

> **Interest fit matters, but it is only one predictor of work happiness. Autonomy, manager/team quality, growth, workload, status/security, meaning, and day-to-day work design often matter as much or more. People also tend to over-predict the effect of the most salient difference between options.**

## What research says

### 1. “Interest fit” predicts job satisfaction, but usually modestly

In vocational psychology, a lot of work studies whether people are happier when their job matches their interests — often using Holland/RIASEC-style interest congruence.

Meta-analyses such as Assouline & Meir 1987, Tranberg et al. 1993, and later reviews generally find that **interest congruence correlates with job satisfaction**, but often only in the **small-to-moderate** range — roughly something like `r ≈ .10–.30`, depending heavily on measurement.

That means interest fit is real, but it does not explain most of the variance in job satisfaction. In plain terms: people whose jobs match their interests tend to be somewhat more satisfied, but many other factors can swamp that.

Also, the marginal difference between:

- “this work is pretty interesting”
- “this work is the most interesting thing I could possibly do”

may be much smaller than the difference between:

- great manager vs bad manager
- high autonomy vs low autonomy
- healthy workload vs constant stress
- real leadership scope vs nominal leadership
- strong team vs frustrating team

### 2. Broader person-job fit matters more than “topic interest”

A meta-analysis by Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 found that broader forms of fit — person-job fit, person-organization fit, person-supervisor fit — are strongly related to satisfaction and commitment.

But “fit” here includes much more than intellectual interest: values, autonomy, role expectations, skills, culture, manager relationship, and reward structure.

So if the Google role gives you something you genuinely want — leading people, shaping direction, building a team — that is not merely an “extrinsic” factor. It could be a major source of fit.

### 3. Work design variables are very important

Work-design research, including Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model and meta-analyses such as Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, consistently finds that satisfaction and motivation are associated with things like:

- autonomy
- feedback
- skill variety
- task significance
- social support
- quality of leadership
- clarity of goals
- manageable workload

This is relevant because “interesting research problem” is only one part of the actual lived job. Your experienced happiness may depend more on your weekly calendar than on the abstract topic.

A less glamorous research area with high autonomy, a strong team, good mentorship, and leadership growth may feel better than a more fascinating area with low control or constant pressure.

### 4. People often mispredict what will make them happy

Affective forecasting research — e.g. work by Wilson & Gilbert — shows that people often overestimate the long-term emotional effect of salient life changes. Kahneman’s “focusing illusion” is relevant here: when you focus on one difference between options, it can loom larger than it will in daily life.

In career decisions, people may over-focus on:

- prestige
- salary
- title
- intellectual glamour
- company brand
- one exciting project

and underweight:

- how many meetings they will have
- who their manager is
- whether they can say no
- whether they like their teammates
- whether they feel respected
- commute/time zone/friction
- how much energy they have after work

So yes: there is research-consistent reason to doubt the folk advice “just pick the most interesting job.”

## How I’d apply this to your case

I would not frame this as:

> OpenAI = interesting  
> Google = money + management  
> therefore which do I value more?

That is too coarse.

Instead, ask:

### Is the Google role “less interesting” or “below your interest threshold”?

There is a big difference between:

1. “Not as frontier/intellectually alive as my current work, but still meaningful and technically respectable.”
2. “I already feel bored thinking about it.”

If it is #1, the leadership opportunity may easily compensate. If it is #2, the extra salary and title may not be enough.

### Is the leadership opportunity real?

“Leading a small team” can mean very different things:

- real technical leadership and roadmap ownership
- people management plus strategic influence
- mostly coordination, planning, and status reporting
- being accountable without real authority

Before deciding, I would ask very concretely:

- What decisions would I own?
- What will my first 6–12 months be judged on?
- How many direct reports?
- How much time coding/researching vs meetings?
- Who sets the roadmap?
- What is the publication/open-source/research freedom situation?
- Can I hire?
- Can I choose technical direction?
- What happens if the team’s priorities change?
- What paths exist to move into a more interesting area later?

If the role gives you genuine agency, that matters a lot.

### Can you get the missing thing at OpenAI?

If the main thing attracting you to Google is team leadership, it may be worth having a direct conversation internally:

> “I’m realizing that leading a small team is a major growth goal for me. What would a path to that look like here over the next 6–18 months?”

If OpenAI can offer a plausible path to leadership while keeping you on more interesting work, that may dominate both options.

## A useful decision heuristic

I’d use a threshold-plus-optimization model:

1. **Interest threshold:** Is the work interesting enough that you will not feel deadened by it?
2. **Autonomy/growth:** Will you gain ownership, leadership, and valuable skills?
3. **People/manager:** Do you trust the manager and like the team?
4. **Career capital:** In two years, which path gives you a better story and better options?
5. **Life quality:** Does the salary/lifestyle difference materially change your life?
6. **Reversibility:** If you leave, can you return to frontier research later? If you stay, can you get leadership later?

My guess: you should not maximize “interestingness” alone. But you also should not dismiss it, especially in research, where intrinsic curiosity fuels persistence and creativity.

## Bottom line

The literature does **not** say “interesting work is unimportant.” It says something closer to:

> Being interested in your work helps, but once the job clears a decent interest threshold, other factors — autonomy, team, manager, growth, meaning, and daily work design — can matter as much or more for happiness.

So if Google is merely “less fascinating” but offers real leadership, autonomy, and growth, it could be the better move. If it is genuinely boring or mostly bureaucratic, the higher salary and title may not compensate.

Given your wording — “leading a small team is something I always wanted to do” — I would take that seriously. That is not a superficial preference. But I’d verify that the Google role truly gives you the kind of leadership you want, and I’d also check whether OpenAI can offer a path to that without making you give up the work you find most alive.

answer:
Yes—there is evidence consistent with your suspicion, but the nuanced answer is:

**Interesting work matters, but “choose the maximally interesting job” is not reliably the same as “choose the happiest life.”** Research tends to find that interest/fit predicts job satisfaction, but the effects are usually **modest-to-moderate**, and other variables—autonomy, manager quality, stress, relationships, meaning, growth, commute, status, hours, psychological safety—often matter as much or more.

### What the research roughly says

1. **Vocational-interest fit helps, but not overwhelmingly.**  
   Meta-analyses of Holland-style “person–environment fit” / vocational interest congruence generally find correlations with job satisfaction in the rough range of **~0.15–0.30**. That is real, but far from determinative. It means people whose jobs match their interests are somewhat more satisfied, but many people in “less ideal-interest” jobs are happier than people in “perfect-interest” jobs because of other factors.

2. **Broader person–job fit matters more than “interestingness.”**  
   Meta-analyses on person–job and person–organization fit find stronger relationships with job satisfaction than narrow interest fit. But “fit” includes things like:  
   - autonomy  
   - values alignment  
   - competence/mastery  
   - workload  
   - social environment  
   - incentives  
   - role clarity  
   - growth opportunities  
   So the question is not just “which work is more intellectually interesting?” but “which environment fits the life and identity I want?”

3. **Job characteristics like autonomy and meaning are very important.**  
   The job characteristics literature—Hackman & Oldham, and later meta-analyses like Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson—finds that autonomy, task significance, feedback, skill variety, and identity of work are strongly associated with satisfaction and motivation.  
   A team-lead role may be less technically interesting but higher in autonomy, impact, mentoring, ownership, and status. Those can compensate a lot.

4. **People often overpredict the happiness effect of salient job attributes.**  
   Affective forecasting research—Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman, etc.—suggests that people tend to focus on the most vivid difference between two futures and overestimate how much it will affect day-to-day happiness.  
   In your case, the vivid axis is probably:  
   **OpenAI = fascinating research** vs. **Google = less interesting but leadership/pay**.  
   But your actual day-to-day happiness may depend more on: your manager, calendar composition, autonomy, stress, bureaucracy, quality of teammates, and whether you feel respected.

5. **“Passion” can be double-edged.**  
   Work-as-calling research finds that people who feel a calling often report more meaning and satisfaction—but also sometimes accept worse boundaries, lower pay, more exploitation, or higher burnout. “Interesting mission” can keep people in roles that are otherwise not optimal.

### So: are people much happier when they choose the most interesting job possible?

**Not necessarily.** The evidence does not support a simple rule like:

> “Maximize intellectual interestingness and happiness follows.”

A better rule is probably:

> “Make sure the job clears a threshold of genuine interest/meaning, then optimize for autonomy, people, growth, stress, values, and long-term trajectory.”

For you, the key distinction may be:

- If the Google role is merely “less technically interesting” but gives you real leadership, ownership, mentorship, and strategic influence, it could make you happier.
- If it is “less interesting” *and* more bureaucratic, politically constrained, or disconnected from work you care about, the higher salary and team lead title may not compensate.
- If your current OpenAI work feels once-in-a-career intellectually alive, that is a serious consideration; rare intrinsic motivation is valuable.
- But if leading a team is something you deeply want, that itself is a form of interesting work—just along a different dimension.

### A practical way to decide

I’d compare the roles on expected weekly life, not abstract prestige/interest:

| Dimension | OpenAI current | Google offer |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intellectual excitement | ? | ? |
| Autonomy | ? | ? |
| Real decision authority | ? | ? |
| Quality of manager | ? | ? |
| Quality of team | ? | ? |
| Stress / hours / sustainability | ? | ? |
| Growth toward desired identity | ? | ? |
| Long-term optionality | ? | ? |
| Mission/values fit | ? | ? |
| Compensation difference after tax | ? | ? |

Also ask Google very concretely:

- What percentage of my time is management vs. technical work?
- Do I have real roadmap authority?
- How is the team evaluated?
- What happens if priorities change?
- Can I hire/fire/performance-manage, or am I just “tech lead with meetings”?
- Who is my manager, and what is their style?
- How much room is there to shape the research/technical direction?

And ask yourself:

> If the salary were identical, would I still be tempted by Google?  
> If the title were identical, would I still be tempted?  
> If OpenAI offered me a small-team leadership path in 6–12 months, would I stay?

That last question is especially important. If what you want is leadership, it may be worth exploring whether you can get that where the work is already highly interesting.

### Bottom line

There is research suggesting that **interest fit matters, but not so much that you should automatically choose the most interesting job.** People are often happier in jobs that are “interesting enough” but better on autonomy, people, leadership, and lifestyle.

In your specific case, I would not frame it as:

> interesting OpenAI vs. boring Google

but rather:

> deep technical fascination vs. leadership growth/ownership.

If becoming a team lead is a central career goal and the Google role is genuinely empowered, it could be a rational move even if the topic is less exciting. If the leadership is shallow or the work feels meaningfully dull, I’d be cautious—the novelty of title and salary may fade faster than the loss of intrinsic motivation.

answer:
Yes — there’s evidence against the *strong* version of “choose the most interesting job possible.” But the evidence does **not** say “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It says something more like:

> Once a job is above a threshold of intrinsic interest, other factors — autonomy, people, management quality, growth, identity, stress, and meaning — often matter as much or more for happiness than maximizing topical interestingness.

### What the research roughly says

#### 1. Interest–job fit predicts satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly
Vocational psychology has studied “person–environment fit” and “interest congruence” for decades. Meta-analyses of Holland-style interest fit generally find that people whose jobs match their interests are more satisfied, but the relationship is usually **small to moderate**, not huge — often correlations around ~0.15–0.30 depending on measurement.

Examples: Assouline & Meir 1987; Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000; Tsabari, Tziner & Meir 2005.

So: interest matters, but it explains only a fraction of job satisfaction.

#### 2. Broader person–job fit matters more than “interesting topic”
A broader construct — “does this job fit my needs, values, abilities, desired lifestyle, and goals?” — is more strongly related to satisfaction. Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 found person–job fit had a strong association with job satisfaction.

That’s important for your case because “leading a small team” may be a real fit variable, not a superficial perk. If you genuinely want management, mentoring, leverage, and responsibility, that may increase satisfaction even if the technical topic is less exciting.

#### 3. Day-to-day job design is a huge deal
Work design research finds that things like:

- autonomy,
- feedback,
- task significance,
- skill variety,
- social support,
- reasonable workload,
- good management,

are strongly tied to satisfaction and motivation. See Hackman & Oldham’s job characteristics model and the meta-analysis by Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007.

This is one reason “interesting topic” can mislead. A job on a fascinating problem with low autonomy, chaotic priorities, bad management, or constant stress can feel worse than a less glamorous job with trust, leverage, and good people.

#### 4. People often overpredict how much a job change will affect happiness
Affective forecasting research — e.g. work by Daniel Gilbert and Timothy Wilson — suggests people often overestimate how much future circumstances will change their day-to-day happiness. This applies to both directions:

- “If I get the more prestigious / better-paid / manager role, I’ll be much happier.”
- “If I leave the most interesting technical work, I’ll be miserable.”

Both might be exaggerated.

#### 5. But for research/creative work, intrinsic interest is unusually important
One caveat: research is not a generic job. Intrinsic motivation is particularly important for creativity, deep work, and scientific contribution. Teresa Amabile’s work on creativity, for example, emphasizes intrinsic motivation as a driver of creative performance.

So if the OpenAI work is not merely “more fun,” but deeply tied to your identity, long-term research taste, career capital, and sense of impact, then the “interestingness” variable deserves extra weight.

### How I’d translate this to your decision

I would not ask:

> Which job is more interesting?

I would ask:

> Is the Google role interesting enough, and does it give me other things I deeply want?

A useful framing is threshold-based:

- If Google is **boring or misaligned**, the higher salary and title probably won’t compensate for long.
- If Google is **less thrilling but still intellectually respectable**, and the team-lead role is real, with autonomy and good people, you might be just as happy or happier.
- If OpenAI is uniquely meaningful and you would feel like you gave up the frontier too early, staying may be right even if Google is objectively attractive.

### Questions I’d try to answer before deciding

1. **Do you actually want management, or the idea of management?**  
   Leading a team means 1:1s, planning, performance issues, ambiguity, meetings, prioritization, conflict, and less hands-on technical work. If that sounds energizing, Google’s offer is more valuable. If it sounds like a status marker, be careful.

2. **How much less interesting is the Google work?**  
   “Less interesting than OpenAI” is not the same as “uninteresting.” Almost everything is less interesting than frontier AI research right now.

3. **Is the Google role truly high-autonomy?**  
   A small-team lead role can be great if you set direction. It can be frustrating if you mostly execute someone else’s roadmap.

4. **Can OpenAI offer a team-lead path?**  
   If leading people is something you strongly want, it’s worth asking whether there is a credible path internally. If yes, staying becomes more attractive.

5. **What would you regret more in two years?**  
   - “I missed my chance to lead a team.”
   - “I left the most interesting technical work of my life.”
   - “I optimized for salary/title and became bored.”
   - “I stayed intellectually stimulated but didn’t grow in leadership.”

### Bottom line

There is research support for being skeptical of “maximize interestingness at all costs.” Interest matters, but happiness at work is usually a bundle: autonomy, competence, relatedness, growth, meaning, stress, status, money, and identity.

For your case, I’d treat “interesting work” as a **threshold requirement**, not the sole objective. If the Google role clears the interest threshold and gives you real leadership, autonomy, and good people, it may be a very reasonable move. If it fails that threshold and you would mostly be taking it for salary/title, I’d be cautious.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there’s evidence that “doing the most interesting possible work” is not as dominant a driver of happiness as people often imply**. But the stronger claim—“interestingness barely matters”—is too strong. The research picture is more like:

> Interesting work helps, but its average effect is modest-to-moderate; autonomy, competence/mastery, relationships, management quality, stress, work-life balance, identity fit, and future optionality often matter as much or more.

A few relevant research threads:

### 1. Interest/job-fit predicts satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly

Vocational psychology has studied “interest congruence”: how well someone’s interests match their occupation. Meta-analyses generally find that this predicts job satisfaction, but the correlations are often in the **~0.15–0.30** range depending on measurement and population.

That is meaningful, but not huge: a correlation of 0.20 explains about 4% of variance. So “this job matches my interests” matters, but it does not determine happiness.

Relevant literature: Holland’s vocational-interest theory; Assouline & Meir 1987; Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993; Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000; Nye et al. 2012.

### 2. Broader person-job fit matters more than “interestingness”

When researchers look at broader **person-job fit**—skills, values, needs, autonomy, rewards, role expectations—the relationship with satisfaction is stronger. Meta-analyses often find moderate-to-large associations between person-job fit and job satisfaction.

So the question may not be:

> “Which job is more intellectually interesting?”

but rather:

> “Which job better fits my needs for autonomy, growth, competence, status, impact, social connection, and preferred daily rhythm?”

A leadership role may be less intellectually fascinating but more satisfying if it gives you autonomy, ownership, mentoring, influence, and a sense of progression.

Relevant: Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 on person-environment fit.

### 3. Work design research: autonomy and social context are huge

The job-characteristics literature finds that features like **autonomy**, **feedback**, **task significance**, **skill variety**, and **social support** are strongly associated with motivation and satisfaction.

A “less interesting” role with high autonomy, good colleagues, clear ownership, and a supportive manager can beat a fascinating role with ambiguity, low agency, bad politics, or chronic stress.

Relevant: Hackman & Oldham’s job characteristics model; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 meta-analysis.

### 4. People are bad at forecasting how much a job feature will affect happiness

Affective forecasting research suggests people often overestimate how much one salient factor will affect their future happiness. When you are deciding, “interestingness” or “salary” or “prestige” becomes focal, and you may overpredict its day-to-day effect.

Kahneman called this the **focusing illusion**:

> “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”

So if you are currently focusing on “this work is less interesting,” you may overweight that. But the reverse is also possible: you might overweight “team lead” or “higher salary” because those are salient.

Relevant: Wilson & Gilbert on affective forecasting; Kahneman et al. on focusing illusion.

### 5. Job changes often have a honeymoon effect

There is also evidence for a “honeymoon-hangover” pattern after job changes: satisfaction often rises initially, then partially regresses as novelty fades and the ordinary reality of the role appears.

So “Google + higher salary + team lead” may feel very attractive now, but some of that may be novelty/status. Similarly, staying in the more interesting research role may feel normal because you have adapted to it.

Relevant: Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005.

---

## How I’d interpret this for your situation

I would not ask only:

> “Which job is more interesting?”

I’d ask:

1. **What will my actual week look like?**  
   Meetings? Writing docs? Coaching? Research? Stakeholder management? Coding? Recruiting?

2. **Am I excited by management itself?**  
   Leading a small team can be deeply satisfying if you enjoy mentoring, setting direction, and multiplying others. But if you mainly want research freedom, “team lead” may become a tax.

3. **Is the Google work merely less interesting, or actively boring?**  
   “Somewhat less interesting but high agency” is different from “I will not care about the problem.”

4. **Does the role increase or decrease future optionality?**  
   A first team-lead role at Google could be strong career capital. But if it moves you away from frontier research you love, that also matters.

5. **Can you get the desired thing without switching?**  
   For example, can you negotiate more leadership/mentorship/scope at OpenAI? If your main draw is “I want to lead a small team,” maybe the best move is to seek that where the work is already interesting.

6. **If you regret the decision in 12 months, why?**  
   For Google: “I became a middle manager on problems I don’t care about.”  
   For staying: “I missed my chance to develop leadership and broaden my career.”  
   Which regret feels more likely and more painful?

---

## Bottom line

The research does support your suspicion: **maximizing for interestingness alone is probably not optimal for happiness**. Interest matters, but average effects are not enormous, and other features—autonomy, mastery, social environment, identity, management quality, and lifestyle—can matter just as much.

For you, the key question is probably not “OpenAI interesting vs Google less interesting.” It is:

> “Do I want my next growth edge to be deeper research, or learning to lead?”

If leadership is a real long-term desire, and the Google role gives genuine ownership rather than bureaucratic coordination, it may be worth serious consideration even if the topic is less exciting. If your core source of energy is being close to the most interesting research problems, then a slightly higher salary and title may not compensate for losing that.

answer:
Short answer: **yes — the research is pretty consistent with “interesting work matters, but not enough to blindly maximize it.”** Interest/fit predicts job satisfaction, but usually only **modestly**; autonomy, social environment, manager quality, workload, meaning, competence growth, and broad person–job fit often matter as much or more.

There is no clean study of “OpenAI frontier-research IC role vs. Google team-lead role,” of course, but several literatures are relevant.

## 1. Interest fit predicts satisfaction, but modestly

The vocational psychology literature — Holland-code / person–environment fit / interest congruence — asks roughly: *are people happier when their job matches their interests?*

Meta-analyses generally find that vocational interest congruence correlates with job satisfaction around **r ≈ .20–.30**, sometimes lower depending on measurement. See e.g.:

- Assouline & Meir, 1987, meta-analysis of person–environment congruence and well-being.
- Spokane, Meir & Catalano, 2000, review of Holland congruence research.
- Nye et al., 2012/2017, meta-analytic work on vocational interests and work outcomes.

An r of .20–.30 is real, but it means interest match explains something like **4–9% of the variance** in job satisfaction. So: not irrelevant, but far from “choose the most intellectually interesting job and happiness follows.”

Also, in your comparison, both roles are likely already in the far upper tail of “interesting technical work” relative to normal jobs. The marginal difference between “extremely interesting” and “less interesting but still intellectually respectable” may be smaller than the raw description makes it feel.

## 2. Broader job design often matters more than “interesting topic”

The job-characteristics literature finds strong links between satisfaction/motivation and features like:

- autonomy,
- feedback,
- task significance,
- skill variety,
- social support,
- quality of management,
- role clarity,
- workload.

Classic sources include Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model and later meta-analyses such as Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.

This is important because “interesting research problem” is only one ingredient. A slightly less fascinating problem with much more autonomy, ownership, mentoring, and influence can easily be better day-to-day than a fascinating problem embedded in frustrating constraints.

In your case, “leading a small team” may satisfy several strong predictors of work well-being: autonomy, competence, impact, status, relatedness, and growth. If that is genuinely something you want — not just a title — it should count as an intrinsic feature, not merely an extrinsic perk.

## 3. People overestimate how much one salient feature will affect happiness

Affective forecasting research — e.g. Gilbert, Wilson and colleagues — shows that people often overpredict the long-term emotional impact of salient changes. They focus on the obvious difference, underweight adaptation, and underweight mundane daily details.

For job choices, the salient feature might be:

- “OpenAI work is more interesting,” or
- “Google gives me a team,” or
- “the salary is higher.”

But after six months, your experienced happiness may be driven more by:

- who your manager is,
- whether your calendar is tolerable,
- whether you respect your teammates,
- whether you get uninterrupted deep-work time,
- whether promotion incentives are sane,
- whether you feel proud of the product/research direction,
- whether you are learning,
- whether your work creates chronic stress.

So the research would warn against deciding based only on the most cognitively salient dimension: “interestingness.”

## 4. There is a dispositional / adaptation component to job satisfaction

Job satisfaction is partly stable across roles. Studies by Staw & Ross and by Judge and colleagues find that people carry a nontrivial dispositional component of satisfaction from job to job. Twin studies such as Arvey et al. also suggest a heritable/stable component.

That does **not** mean jobs don’t matter. They do. But it does mean that “if I choose the perfectly interesting job, I’ll be much happier” is often an overestimate. Some people remain restless even in objectively amazing roles; others are fairly satisfied across many decent environments.

## 5. Money helps, but at your likely income level it may be secondary

Income and well-being are positively related, especially on a log scale. Recent work by Killingsworth and others suggests well-being continues to rise with income for many people, though marginal returns diminish.

For a senior research/engineering role at OpenAI vs Google, the salary delta may matter less than it would for someone moving from financial stress to security. It can still matter if it buys concrete things: freedom, family support, risk tolerance, future optionality, location, etc. But “a bit higher salary” should probably not dominate the decision unless it changes your life in a specific way.

## My read for your specific choice

I would not frame it as:

> interesting work vs. boring management + money.

I’d frame it as:

> frontier technical interest and current trajectory  
> vs.  
> leadership growth, ownership, compensation, and possibly less frontier-level technical content.

The research does **not** say “take the most interesting job.” It says something closer to:

> Choose the job with the best total package of autonomy, competence growth, social environment, meaning, manageable stress, and fit with your medium-term identity.

If leading a team is something you have “always wanted to do,” then the Google offer may be more psychologically important than its project description suggests. But only if the role gives you real authority, real mentorship opportunities, and a team/problem you can care about.

## Useful questions to ask before deciding

I’d ask Google very concretely:

1. **What fraction of my time is expected to be management vs. IC technical work?**
2. **Will I have real decision authority, or just coordination responsibility?**
3. **Who is my manager, and what are their expectations?**
4. **How strong is the team? Am I inheriting dysfunction?**
5. **What does success look like after 6 and 12 months?**
6. **Can I transfer internally if the project is not a fit?**
7. **How much deep technical work can I still do?**
8. **Is the project less interesting only compared with OpenAI, or actually unmotivating?**
9. **What would make me regret leaving OpenAI?**
10. **What would make me regret not trying leadership now?**

A good heuristic: imagine your weekly calendar after the novelty wears off. Not the offer letter, not the prestige, not the abstract research agenda — the actual calendar.

If the Google calendar looks energizing because of ownership, mentorship, and broader impact, the “less interesting” label may be overweighted. If it looks like meetings, politics, and technical distance from things you love, then the small salary bump and title probably won’t compensate.

So yes: there is evidence against simply maximizing “interestingness.” But there is also evidence that fit, autonomy, growth, and meaning matter a lot. The real question is whether the Google role is less interesting **only intellectually**, or whether it is less aligned with the kind of person you want to become.

answer:
Short answer: yes, there’s research suggesting that “pick the maximally interesting job” is not a reliable recipe for much higher happiness. But the stronger conclusion is **not** “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It’s more like:

> Interest matters, but it is only one part of work well-being. Autonomy, competence/growth, social environment, meaning/impact, manager quality, and fit with your desired identity often matter as much or more. Also, people are not great at forecasting how much happier one job will make them.

Some relevant findings:

### 1. Vocational “interest fit” predicts satisfaction, but modestly
Meta-analyses in vocational psychology find that congruence between someone’s interests and their occupation is positively related to job satisfaction, but the effect is usually **modest**, not overwhelming. For example, studies around Holland-style interest congruence generally find correlations in the rough ballpark of **r ≈ .10–.30**, depending on method and population.

Interpretation: if you love research-y technical work, being in a research-y technical job helps. But it does not dominate everything else.

### 2. Broader person–job fit and work design matter a lot
The stronger predictors are often broader than “is the topic interesting?” Meta-analyses of job design show that satisfaction and motivation are strongly associated with things like:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- good social support,
- clear responsibility/ownership.

This is related to Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model and later meta-analyses like Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007.

So a “less intellectually fascinating” job that gives you real ownership, leadership, autonomy, learning, and good people may be surprisingly satisfying. Conversely, a fascinating research area with low agency, bad politics, or no growth can become miserable.

### 3. People who maximize for “the best job” can feel worse
A particularly relevant result: Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz 2006 studied graduating students entering the job market. “Maximizers” — people trying to find the absolute best option — ended up with **higher salaries**, but reported **lower satisfaction, more stress, and more regret** than “satisficers.”

This doesn’t mean optimization is bad. But it suggests that searching for the “maximally interesting / maximally prestigious / maximally optimal” job can create comparison and regret dynamics that reduce happiness.

### 4. Pay matters, but marginal salary increases matter less at high income
Meta-analytic work, e.g. Judge et al. 2010, finds that pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. At already-high compensation levels, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely to move day-to-day happiness much unless it changes your financial freedom, family situation, risk tolerance, or ability to pursue future goals.

### 5. People adapt, and they mispredict day-to-day experience
Affective forecasting research broadly shows that people overestimate how much major choices will change their long-term happiness. In jobs, the day-to-day texture often matters more than the headline: who you work with, how much agency you have, whether meetings drain you, whether you feel effective, whether you are learning.

So for your specific case, I would not reduce it to:

> OpenAI = interesting  
> Google = less interesting but more money/management

The real question is probably:

> Which role gives me more of the psychological nutrients I want over the next 2–4 years?

For you, “leading a small team” sounds like it may be intrinsically important, not merely a résumé/status thing. Management/leadership can be deeply interesting if you enjoy strategy, mentoring, prioritization, and shaping an agenda. But if the Google work is genuinely much less meaningful to you, the novelty of leading could wear off and leave you missing the research frontier.

A practical way to decide:

1. **Separate “topic interest” from “role interest.”**  
   You may find frontier research more interesting as a topic, but team leadership more interesting as a role.

2. **Ask what your calendar looks like.**  
   Not “what is the mission statement?” but: in a normal week, how many hours are deep technical work, meetings, mentoring, planning, coding, reading papers, firefighting?

3. **Estimate learning rate.**  
   Which job makes you more formidable in two years? As a technical researcher? As a leader? As both?

4. **Consider reversibility.**  
   Is it easier to go from OpenAI research → Google/team lead later, or Google/team lead → frontier research later? The answer may depend heavily on your subfield and network.

5. **Try to negotiate the missing piece.**  
   Before switching, ask whether OpenAI can give you more leadership scope. Or ask Google whether the role can include a stronger technical/research component.

My read from the research: don’t blindly choose the most “interesting” work, because interest alone is not a complete predictor of happiness. But also don’t dismiss your current work’s interestingness as a fake luxury; intrinsic motivation and meaningful work are real. If the salary bump is modest, the key tradeoff is probably **frontier technical interest vs. desired leadership growth**, not money.

answer:
Short version: **yes, there’s evidence against “just choose the most interesting work” as a universal happiness rule**. Interesting work matters, but research generally finds it is **one moderate predictor among many**, not a dominant determinant of long-run happiness.

A few relevant findings:

1. **Interest/job fit predicts satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly.**  
   Vocational-interest congruence — doing work that matches your interests — is usually associated with higher job satisfaction, but meta-analytic effects are often **modest to moderate**, roughly in the “helpful but not destiny” range. Broader person-job fit matters more, but that includes autonomy, values, pay, working conditions, skill use, coworkers, etc., not just “this topic is fascinating.”

2. **Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are related, but imperfectly.**  
   Meta-analyses find job satisfaction correlates with overall life satisfaction around **r ≈ .4**. That’s meaningful, but it also means a lot of life happiness is driven by non-job factors, stable personality, relationships, health, time use, stress, sleep, etc. Even a much “better” job may not transform overall happiness as much as one imagines.

3. **Daily experience often beats abstract “interestingness.”**  
   Work-design research finds that autonomy, feedback, task significance, social support, low role conflict, good management, and manageable workload are big predictors of engagement and satisfaction. A job that is less intellectually sexy but gives you ownership, autonomy, a good team, and leadership growth can easily feel better day-to-day than a fascinating job with frustration, politics, ambiguity, or little agency.

4. **People are bad at forecasting how much one salient difference will matter.**  
   Affective-forecasting and “focusing illusion” research suggests we overweight the most salient contrast — e.g. “frontier AI research vs. less interesting Google work” — and underweight the mundane texture of daily life: meetings, manager quality, energy after work, colleagues, commute, bureaucratic drag, how often you feel effective.

5. **At high income levels, extra salary is probably not the main driver.**  
   Income still matters, especially for security/options, but at OpenAI/Google compensation levels, the happiness impact of a moderate raise is likely smaller than differences in autonomy, stress, meaning, learning, and quality of colleagues.

So I’d frame your choice less as:

> “Should I choose the more interesting job?”

and more as:

> “Which job gives me the better overall bundle of autonomy, growth, meaning, relationships, competence, energy, future options, and day-to-day motivation?”

For your specific case, the crucial ambiguity is this:

- If “less interesting at Google” means **less frontier-research excitement but more ownership, people leadership, autonomy, mentorship, and organizational leverage**, it could plausibly make you as happy or happier.
- If it means **boring product work, slower pace, more bureaucracy, less agency, and management responsibilities without real authority**, then the salary/title bump may not compensate.

A useful exercise: forecast a normal Tuesday in each role. Hour by hour. Deep work? 1:1s? Roadmapping? Paper/model discussions? Stakeholder management? Hiring? Debugging? Meetings? Politics? Then ask: “Which Tuesday would I rather live 200 times per year?”

Also, since leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted, don’t discount that as “less interesting.” Management can be intrinsically interesting in a different way: strategy, people development, leverage, taste-building, prioritization. The question is whether you want to become interested in that craft enough to trade off some research-content excitement.

My read from the literature: **do not blindly maximize intellectual interestingness**. But also don’t dismiss it. Treat it as one large term in a multi-variable objective, alongside autonomy, leadership growth, quality of manager/team, stress, meaning, and future optionality.

answer:
Short answer: **there isn’t a clean result saying “people who choose the most interesting job are not happier.”** The research mostly says something subtler:

> **Intrinsic interest matters, but it is not the only—or always the strongest—driver of work happiness.** Autonomy, competence/growth, relationships, manager quality, workload, meaning, fairness, commute, and work-life fit often matter as much or more. Also, people are not great at forecasting which job will make them happy.

So “OpenAI = more interesting topic” should be taken seriously, but not as a trump card. A Google role with **more autonomy, leadership, mentoring, scope, better manager, less stress, and better life fit** could plausibly make you happier even if the subject matter is less thrilling.

### What the research suggests

1. **Interesting/intrinsic work does predict job satisfaction.**  
   Work-design research—e.g. Hackman & Oldham’s job characteristics model and meta-analyses like Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007—finds that autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback, and task identity are all associated with higher job satisfaction and motivation. So the folk advice is not baseless.

2. **But “interest fit” alone is usually only a moderate predictor.**  
   Studies of vocational interest/person-job fit generally find positive but imperfect relationships with satisfaction and performance. Broad person-job fit matters a lot, but “the topic is maximally interesting to me” is only one component of that fit.

3. **Autonomy and social environment are huge.**  
   Self-determination theory—Deci & Ryan—suggests that well-being at work depends heavily on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A role where you lead a small team could score highly on all three: more control, growth as a leader, and meaningful mentoring relationships.

4. **People adapt, and affective forecasting is noisy.**  
   Research by Gilbert, Wilson, and others on affective forecasting suggests people often overestimate how much a future condition will affect their happiness. “This topic is so interesting” or “this salary is higher” may be less emotionally durable than expected. Day-to-day texture often dominates: meetings, manager, teammates, stress, bureaucracy, hours, commute, decision rights.

5. **Salary helps, but at your likely income level it may not dominate.**  
   Meta-analyses such as Judge et al. 2010 find pay has a positive but modest relationship with job satisfaction. At high compensation levels, extra salary matters more through security, freedom, fairness, and optionality than through daily happiness.

### Applied to your situation

The key distinction is:

- **Interesting domain:** frontier AI research, intellectually exciting problems, being close to the action.
- **Interesting role:** leadership, ownership, people development, strategy, influence, team-building.

You may be comparing “interesting technical content” against “interesting career shape.” If leading a small team is something you have genuinely wanted for a long time, that is not a trivial perk—it is itself an intrinsic motivator.

I would not ask, “Which job is more interesting?”  
I would ask:

> “On an ordinary Tuesday, which job gives me more energy, agency, learning, and pride?”

Some concrete questions to answer:

| Dimension | OpenAI role | Google role |
|---|---:|---:|
| Technical fascination | likely higher | lower |
| Autonomy | ? | maybe higher |
| Leadership growth | maybe lower | higher |
| Team quality | ? | ? |
| Manager quality | ? | ? |
| Bureaucracy/friction | ? | potentially higher |
| Pace/stress | ? | ? |
| Long-term optionality | frontier research credibility | management/TL credential |
| Alignment with identity | “I work on the most interesting stuff” | “I build and lead teams” |

### My practical advice

Before deciding, I’d try to create a third option:

1. **Tell OpenAI you want to lead.**  
   Not as a threat, but honestly: “I’m excited by my work here, but I’m realizing team leadership is important to me. Is there a path to leading a small group or taking on more technical/team ownership?”  
   If OpenAI can offer more leadership while preserving the interesting work, that may dominate.

2. **Interrogate the Google role hard.**  
   Ask:
   - How much time is people management vs technical work?
   - How much decision authority will I actually have?
   - What are the first 6–12 month goals?
   - Who is my manager, and how do they operate?
   - Can I shape the team’s research/technical direction?
   - How easy is internal transfer if the work is too boring?
   - Is this real leadership or mostly coordination/project management?

3. **Do a regret pre-mortem.**  
   Imagine you take Google and regret it. Why? Probably: boredom, bureaucracy, loss of frontier relevance, less technical depth.  
   Imagine you stay at OpenAI and regret it. Why? Probably: missed leadership opportunity, feeling stuck as an IC, compensation/leveling frustration.  
   Which regret feels more likely and more painful?

### Bottom line

The evidence does **not** support “always choose the most interesting job possible.” It supports something more like:

> Choose work that is sufficiently interesting, gives you autonomy and growth, surrounds you with good people, and fits the life/career you want.

If the Google role is merely “higher salary plus boring work,” I’d be cautious.  
If it is “slightly less fascinating topic, but real leadership, autonomy, great team, and a skill path you deeply want,” then taking it could be a very rational happiness-maximizing move.

But first I’d see whether you can get the leadership path at OpenAI. That sounds like the potentially best version: **interesting work plus the growth dimension you’re missing.**

answer:
Short version: **yes, there is evidence consistent with “don’t maximize only for interestingness.”** Interesting work matters, but the measured relationship between *interest fit* and happiness/job satisfaction is usually **positive but modest**, and other factors—autonomy, manager quality, team culture, stress, growth, meaning, work-life balance—can easily dominate.

### What the research roughly says

1. **Interest fit predicts job satisfaction, but not hugely.**  
   The closest literature is “person–job/person–environment fit” and “vocational interest congruence.” Meta-analyses generally find that people are more satisfied when their work matches their interests, but the correlations are often around **r ≈ .15–.25** depending on measures. That is real, but it explains only a small fraction of variance in job satisfaction.  
   References: Assouline & Meir 1987; Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993; Spokane 1985.

2. **Work design factors are also very important.**  
   The job-characteristics literature finds that autonomy, feedback, task significance, social support, role clarity, workload, and control over one’s work are strong predictors of satisfaction and motivation. “Interesting tasks” are one piece of a larger bundle.  
   Reference: Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 meta-analysis.

3. **People adapt to job changes more than they expect.**  
   There is a “honeymoon-hangover” pattern: job satisfaction often jumps after a move or promotion, then partially fades. This suggests that a salient feature—more exciting work, higher pay, title, prestige—may not create as much durable happiness as forecast.  
   Reference: Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005.

4. **People overfocus on the salient difference.**  
   Affective forecasting research suggests we often overestimate how much one prominent dimension will affect our future wellbeing. In your case, “OpenAI = more interesting” vs “Google = lead a team / higher pay” may be too compressed. Your actual happiness will depend on the texture of ordinary Tuesdays.  
   Reference: Wilson & Gilbert on affective forecasting; Kahneman’s “focusing illusion.”

### How I’d apply that to your case

I would not read the evidence as “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It clearly does. But I would read it as:

> Once a job clears a threshold of being intellectually engaging, the marginal benefit of making it even more interesting may be smaller than people assume.

Especially for someone already doing elite AI/research-engineering work, both options may be far above the “interesting enough” threshold. Then factors like **agency, learning trajectory, leadership growth, team quality, stress, and future optionality** may matter more.

Also, “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk. If you have always wanted to do it, that may be a deep intrinsic preference: autonomy, mentorship, leverage, responsibility, and impact through others. It may be “interesting” in a different way from technical novelty.

### A useful framing

Ask yourself:

- Is the Google work **less interesting**, or **not interesting enough**? Those are very different.
- Is the team-lead role real authority, or mostly accountability without control?
- Would you enjoy spending 40–60% of your time on coordination, hiring, reviews, planning, and ambiguity?
- At OpenAI, is there a plausible path to leadership soon?
- At Google, is there a plausible path back toward more frontier/interesting work if you miss it?
- Which role gives you more autonomy over what you work on?
- Which environment has the manager/team you would rather be around on a bad week?

My rough heuristic would be:

- If Google is “7/10 interesting” plus real leadership, good manager, higher autonomy, and better life setup: **do not reject it just because OpenAI is 10/10 interesting**.
- If Google is “3/10 interesting” and the leadership is mostly meetings, bureaucracy, and roadmap execution without real agency: **the evidence does not save that tradeoff**.
- If OpenAI feels like a once-in-a-career mission fit, not merely “cooler technical work,” weight that separately from interestingness.

So yes: the research supports skepticism toward “always pick the most interesting job.” A better target is probably **sufficiently interesting work + high autonomy + strong people + growth in the direction you want**.

For OpenAI, I’d want to understand the leadership path and responsibilities, while maybe keeping internal roles less emphasized. I’ll craft the final answer to be informative but not overwhelming, possibly using bullets and aiming for a moderate verbosity in my response.
answer:
Short answer: **yes — the research supports skepticism about the strong claim “pick the most interesting work possible and you’ll be much happier.”** But it does **not** support “interestingness doesn’t matter.” A better summary is:

> Work that fits your interests and feels intrinsically motivating matters, but the marginal happiness gain from “even more interesting” work is often smaller than people expect, especially once both options are already good. Day-to-day autonomy, manager quality, workload, social context, competence growth, and whether the role matches your broader goals can matter as much or more.

A useful distinction:

1. **Weak claim:** “Don’t take work you find boring/meaningless.”  
   → Strongly plausible and supported.

2. **Strong claim:** “Among good jobs, choose the most intellectually interesting one; it will make you much happier.”  
   → Much less clearly supported.

## What the research says

### 1. Interest fit predicts job satisfaction, but usually modestly

Research on **person–environment fit** and **vocational interest congruence** — e.g. Holland/RIASEC-style matching — generally finds that people are more satisfied when their work fits their interests. But the effect sizes are often not huge.

Meta-analyses such as Assouline & Meir 1987, Spokane et al. 2000, and later work on vocational-interest fit tend to find correlations between interest congruence and satisfaction/well-being in the rough range of **~0.15–0.30**, depending on methods.

That is meaningful, but not deterministic. It means interest fit matters, but plenty of people in “perfectly matched” jobs are unhappy, and plenty of people in imperfectly matched jobs are happy because other job features are excellent.

Also, those studies usually compare people across fairly broad occupations. Your two options are not “AI research vs. tax accounting.” They are probably both high-status, high-skill technical/research-adjacent roles. In that narrower range, the marginal effect of “more interesting topic” may be smaller.

### 2. Broad person–job fit matters a lot — but “interest” is only one component

A classic meta-analysis by Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction. But person–job fit includes many things:

- Do you use your strengths?
- Are the demands reasonable?
- Does the role give you what you want?
- Is there autonomy?
- Do you like the people?
- Does the job match your career identity?
- Do you feel effective?

So if the Google role gives you leadership, autonomy, mentorship, scope, and a clearer growth path, it may be a better “fit” even if the technical content is less exciting.

### 3. Work design variables are very important

Research on the **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses, e.g. Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, finds that job satisfaction is strongly related to things like:

- Autonomy
- Feedback
- Skill variety
- Task significance
- Social support
- Role clarity
- Manager/team quality

This is relevant because “leading a small team” may increase some of these: autonomy, significance, mentoring, scope. But it may also reduce others: deep technical work, flow, control of your calendar, time for research.

So the important question is not only:

> “Is the subject matter more interesting?”

but also:

> “What will my average Tuesday feel like?”

A less fascinating domain with high autonomy, good people, and meaningful leadership can be happier than a fascinating domain with chaos, low agency, or constant pressure.

### 4. People overpredict the impact of salient differences

Affective forecasting research — Wilson & Gilbert, Kahneman and others — shows that people often overweight the most salient difference between options and underweight mundane repeated experiences.

In career decisions, the salient difference might be:

- “OpenAI work is more interesting”
- “Google salary is higher”
- “Google gives me a team”

But long-run happiness may depend more on repeated daily factors:

- Do I respect my manager?
- Do I get enough uninterrupted time?
- Am I learning?
- Do I feel trusted?
- Are meetings draining me?
- Do I like the people I interact with every day?
- Do I feel proud of what I’m building?

This is why “interestingness” can be overvalued if it is treated as the only variable.

### 5. Salary matters, but probably less at your level

Income is related to life satisfaction, roughly logarithmically: extra money helps, but each additional dollar matters less as income rises. For someone already in a high-paying research engineering role, a “somewhat higher salary” may matter mainly through:

- Financial security
- Status
- Reduced stress
- Optionality
- Feeling fairly valued

Those are real. But unless the difference is life-changing, salary alone is unlikely to dominate daily job quality.

## How I’d apply this to your choice

I would not ask, “Which job is more interesting?” I’d ask:

### 1. Is the Google role above your “interest threshold”?

There is a big difference between:

- “Less interesting than frontier OpenAI work, but still intellectually respectable”
- “I would be bored, cynical, and disengaged”

If it is the first, the leadership opportunity may compensate. If it is the second, be very careful. Chronic boredom is not trivial.

### 2. Is the leadership real?

“Leading a small team” can mean very different things:

- Real ownership of direction, hiring, mentoring, and execution
- Or being a project coordinator with accountability but little power

Before deciding, I’d ask Google very concretely:

- How many reports?
- Will they be direct reports or dotted-line?
- Who sets the roadmap?
- How much technical authority will I have?
- What percent of my week is expected to be hands-on?
- What does success look like after 6 and 12 months?
- Can I shape the technical agenda?
- What happens if the project direction changes?
- Is transfer to more interesting work possible later?

If the role gives you real autonomy and management experience, that is valuable. If it mostly gives you meetings and responsibility without agency, less so.

### 3. Can you get the leadership path at OpenAI?

Since you say leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted, I would also ask internally:

- Is there a path to tech lead / manager / project lead?
- Could you mentor junior researchers or engineers?
- Could you own a subteam or initiative?
- Could your compensation or scope be adjusted?

If OpenAI can give you both interesting work and a plausible leadership path, that changes the comparison.

### 4. Think in terms of two-year regret

Imagine two years from now.

If you stay at OpenAI, possible regret:

> “I kept doing fascinating work, but I still haven’t gotten leadership experience.”

If you go to Google, possible regret:

> “I got management experience and more money, but I miss the frontier and feel intellectually underfed.”

Which regret feels more likely and more painful?

## My read of the research-based answer

The evidence does **not** say: “Always choose the most interesting job.”

It says something more like:

> Choose a job that is sufficiently interesting, uses your strengths, gives you autonomy, has good people, and moves you toward the life/career identity you want. Do not maximize intellectual fascination at the expense of all other variables.

So in your case:

- If the Google work is **still interesting enough**, the leadership opportunity is real, and you genuinely want to build management skill, taking it could be very reasonable.
- If the work is **much less interesting in a way that would make you disengaged**, and the leadership scope is ambiguous, staying with the more exciting work may be wiser.
- If your deepest long-term goal is to become a technical leader/manager, do not dismiss the Google option merely because the topic is less exciting. Leadership itself may be the interesting part.

A good heuristic: **optimize for “high average day quality + long-term skill growth,” not for maximum abstract interestingness.**
